answer:
Take it — but only if you want the venture path.

Math: 8%/month ≈ 2.5x/year. $30k MRR → ~$75k MRR in 12 months. At $12M post you're selling ~17% at ~33x current ARR — a fair-to-good seed price. Solid fund, default-alive leverage: you won't get better terms by waiting a few months unless growth accelerates.

Reasons to take:
- 14 months runway is thin cushion; "default-alive if growth holds" means one bad quarter (churn spike, macro) puts you fundraising from weakness.
- $2M lets you hire ahead of growth; 8% compounding is good but capital could push it to 12–15% if constrained by hiring, not demand.
- Dilution is modest; you keep control at seed.

Reasons to pass:
- You're implicitly signing up for Series A expectations: ~3x/year growth, next raise in 18–24 months, exit >$100M to matter. If a $10–30M/year cash-flowing business is your goal, this trade is bad.
- If growth is not capital-constrained (sales-led? no — if it's organic/product-led and money can't buy growth), the round mostly buys insurance, expensive at 17%.

Decision rule: know what you'd spend the $2M on that moves growth. Concrete plan → take it. "Runway extension" only → negotiate a smaller round or keep bootstrapping.

One caveat worth checking: terms, not just price — board seat, liquidation pref (1x non-participating only), pro-rata. A "solid fund" with clean terms tips this to yes.
